### 2026-07-03 BlackThrush — SURFACED (not landed): merge on >2 WIDE i64 keys falls to Scalar composite path (~0.49-0.93x)
merge_dataframes_on(left, right, ["k0","k1","k2"], Inner) with 3 WIDE i64 key columns is a genuine loss. The dense-packed multi-key path (dense_packed_int64_inner_positions, fp-join ~8082) bails because the mixed-radix product span overflows on a wide component; the typed 2-key path (typed_wide_two_i64_key_positions, ~8095) is u128-2-key-ONLY. So 3+ wide keys fall to collect_composite_keys (fp-join ~8111) = a Vec<CompositeJoinKey> (Vec<SmallVec<[JoinKeyComponent;1]>>) materialized per row — ~2M SmallVec allocs at 1M x (3 keys) — then FxHashMap build/probe over the boxed composites.

MEASURED (1M x 1M, keys k0 card ~700k *7919, k1/k2 card ~400 *primes, ~1M output rows, pandas 2.2.3), HEAVILY LOADED box (loadavg 60-65):
| merge INNER 3 wide-i64 keys | fp | pandas | ratio |
| --- | ---: | ---: | ---: |
| COLD one-shot (fresh frames) | 1088ms | 1016ms | 0.93x LOSS |
| WARM amortized (frame reuse, OnceLock caches key .values()) | 505ms | 246ms | 0.49x LOSS (2x) |
fp loses BOTH ways — even WITH the OnceLock .values() caching helping the warm case, it's 2x pandas. (Per the join memory: bench one-shot, not amortized — the cold 0.93x is the real-world number; the 0.49x warm shows fp is slow even past the materialization.)

FIX PLAN (turnkey, mirrors the 2-key u128 win e47cf3ea7/58906e4ab): generalize typed_wide_two_i64_key_positions to K keys. Read each key col via as_i64_slice (gate: all-valid Int64/Datetime64/Timedelta64, no nulls). Either (a) per-column factorize to a dense gid (0..card_c) then pack into u64/u128 when the cardinality product fits (k0~700k * k1~400 * k2~400 = 1.1e11 < 2^64 here), or (b) hash the K-tuple into a u64 seed with an exact SmallVec<[i64;K]> tie-break map. Build right positions, probe left; emit typed (l,r) positions bypassing collect_composite_keys. INNER first (no null-fill); LEFT/RIGHT add null-fill; OUTER needs argsort by the SEMANTIC decoded (i64,..,i64) tuple (NOT packed order — negatives mis-sort, see the 2-key outer note). Bit-identical target: 3-way differential with WIDE+NEGATIVE keys + disjoint sides. Not landed this turn: correct general-K inner/left/right/outer + outer sort is substantial and correctness-critical; deferred rather than rush a core join path under box contention.

### 2026-07-03 BlackThrush — PARTIAL (landed, still sub-parity): typed general-K merge key path for >2 wide-i64 keys
Followed up the surfaced 3-key gap (9cb9cf6ed). Added typed_wide_multi_i64_key_positions (fp-join): factorize each key column over left∪right into dense first-seen gids, pack each row's per-column gids into ONE u128 (col0 most-significant; bijection when summed gid-bit-widths <= 127, else bail), then reuse the exact 2-key inner/left/outer emit. OUTER argsorts groups by the SEMANTIC original (i64,..,i64) tuple captured at gid creation. Wired after the 2-key path in the merge dispatch. Bit-identical: VERIFIED vs pandas 2.2.3 (3-key inner/left/outer, WIDE ×1e6 keys forcing this path, NEGATIVES + disjoint sides = unmatched both) EXACT; fp-join 138/0.

MEASURED 1M x 1M 3 wide keys, min-of-many, load ~20-27, pandas 2.2.3:
| merge INNER 3 wide-i64 keys | fp before (Scalar composite) | fp after (typed) | pandas | ratio after |
| --- | ---: | ---: | ---: | ---: |
| warm/amortized | ~505ms | ~343ms | ~230ms | 0.65x (was 0.49x) |
fp-side ~1.5x faster (removes the ~2M-alloc Vec<CompositeJoinKey> materialization), but STILL BELOW pandas parity — NOT a pandas win. RESIDUAL FLOOR (surfaced, next levers): (1) the ~1M-row merge OUTPUT BUILD dominates the remaining time (shared by all key paths, likely Scalar per-cell gather vs pandas vectorized take); (2) the per-column FxHashMap factorize is slower than pandas klib khash — the [[openaddr-i64-khash-floor]] open-addr i64 table (~2.3x over FxHashMap) would cut the factorize. Landed anyway as a correctness-preserving strict improvement to a documented-loss hot path; parity needs the output-build lever.

### 2026-07-03 BlackThrush — CORRECTION to the >2-key merge residual (output build is ALREADY typed; floor is khash factorize)
Investigated the "merge output build" lever I surfaced in cc6b9f83d as the residual for the 3-key wide merge. FINDING: that hypothesis was WRONG. The merge output assembly (fp-join ~8501+) routes every all-present (inner) column through take_positions_typed -> Column::take_positions, which for all-valid Float64/Int64 payloads is ALREADY a typed gather (fp-columnar ~8871: contiguous Arc-view / strided / owned-Vec move, NOT a Scalar per-cell clone). Key cols use the i64 arm of take_positions_typed. So the output build is NOT the Scalar floor.

The real residual for the 3-key wide merge (fp ~343ms vs pandas ~230ms = 0.65x) is the per-column FxHashMap<i64,u32> FACTORIZE inside typed_wide_multi_i64_key_positions — 3 cols x ~2M inserts vs pandas klib khash. This is the SAME khash floor documented for SeriesGroupBy wide-i64 keys and value_counts wide-i64 — pandas' khash is ~7ns/row and genuinely hard to beat. The [[openaddr-i64-khash-floor]] open-addr i64 table (~2.3x over FxHashMap) is the only remaining lever and would cut factorize ~50-70ms → ~fp 280ms, likely STILL ~0.8x (not a flip). Conclusion: the 3-key wide merge is now in documented-hard khash-floor territory like the other wide-i64 hash ops; the typed key path (0.49x→0.65x) is the winnable portion. Do NOT re-chase the output build — it is already typed.

### 2026-07-03 BlackThrush — SATURATION SWEEP + scoped read_csv lever (surface)
Swept for a fresh winnable lever; the readily-accessible op surface is SATURATED. Verified-already-typed/optimized this pass (code-read, no re-chase): str.contains (SIMD memmem whole-buffer scan), str.len/upper/lower/slice (apply_str_int/apply_str_utf8 contiguous-Utf8 typed paths), str.extract (native regex — pandas .str.extract is Python-loop, fp wins), df.drop_duplicates/duplicated MULTI-column (TypedDedupCol raw-slice digest, not Scalar), Series.interpolate (typed f64 gap-fill), map/replace/between (typed dense/hash paths), Datetime64 groupby keys (raw-ns dense bypass). The merge output build is also already typed (prior entry). Numeric-bandwidth losses (abs 0.35x, round 0.84x) are the documented-structural [[f64-arc-copy-on-produce]] tax (DEFERRED, needs a Vec-backed all-valid ScalarValues variant). Wide-i64 hash ops (SeriesGroupBy, value_counts, 3-key merge factorize) are khash-floor-bound.

REMAINING HIGH-VALUE LEVER = read_csv on a MIXED CSV (0.59x, the biggest gap; [[read-csv-mixed-parse-gap]]). ROOT: CsvTypedColumnValues (fp-io ~675) has only Int64/Float64 — a mixed CSV abandons the typed per-column parser (per_column: Vec<Vec<CsvTypedColumnValues>>, ~1106) and falls to the generic per-cell parse_scalar + Vec<Scalar> round-trip. SCOPED FIX (multi-turn, shared/hot crate — do NOT rush): add a Utf8 variant to CsvTypedColumnValues; single-pass per-column typed parse keeping the already-shipped contiguous raw bytes+offsets, and on numeric->text promotion mid-column, materialize the Utf8 column FROM THE RAW (preserves verbatim "05"/"5.0" — the correctness crux). Numeric columns then stay typed even when a sibling column is Utf8, eliminating their Scalar round-trip. Needs the full CSV edge-case suite (quoting/escaping/NA/verbatim) green before landing. NOT landed this turn: a correct parser rewrite in a hot crate is not a safe rush; surfaced with the tractable design so a focused fp-io session can execute it.

### 2026-07-03 BlackThrush — read_csv MIXED-CSV typed-per-column accumulator — LANDED WIN (0.70-0.80x -> 0.98-1.02x)
Executed the read_csv lever surfaced in 4679378b3. read_csv_str_uncached (the mixed-CSV path) parsed EVERY cell to a Scalar (parse_scalar + Vec<Scalar> + a from_values re-scan) even for purely-numeric columns sitting next to one Utf8 column. Replaced with a single-pass per-column typed accumulator (Int64/Float64/Fallback): a pure-numeric-no-null column emits its typed column directly (from_i64_values / from_f64_values), and the INSTANT a cell is null/bool/non-numeric-text (an AMBIGUOUS promotion — probed: fp from_values keeps int+null as NULLABLE Int64, coerces int+bool to Int64) the column drops to Fallback and is rebuilt by the UNCHANGED Scalar path from the already-captured verbatim raw bytes. Bit-identical to the old path (proven by analysis + full suite): fp-io 605/0, CSV conformance green.

COLD parse (distinct input/iter so the parse cache never hits), min-of-many, load ~14-18, pandas 2.2.3:
| workload | fp before (Scalar) | fp after (typed) | pandas | ratio |
| --- | ---: | ---: | ---: | ---: |
| read_csv 500k x 3 mixed (int/float/str) | 139.7ms | 114.4ms | 112.2ms | 0.80x -> 0.98x (parity) |
| read_csv 500k x 6 (5 numeric + 1 str) | 257.6ms | 177.1ms | 181.3ms | 0.70x -> 1.02x WIN |
Numeric-heavy (the common real CSV shape) now BEATS pandas; canonical 3-col mixed reaches parity. The more numeric columns, the bigger the win (each skips the Scalar round-trip). Was "the biggest remaining pandas gap". RESIDUAL: nullable-numeric + Utf8 columns still take the Scalar fallback (raw building for all columns remains); a 2-pass classify-then-parse could lift those too — deferred, lower-EV now that the gap is closed to parity.

### 2026-07-03 BlackThrush — read_csv NULLABLE-numeric columns typed too — LOSS 0.70x -> 0.95x (near-parity)
Follow-on to bfbe0e397. That commit fast-pathed only pure-numeric-NO-null columns; a numeric column with any missing value still hit the Scalar fallback, so a CSV with missing data in numeric columns stayed a loss (500k x 5, 4 numeric @10% null + 1 str: fp 212ms vs pandas 148.6ms = 0.70x). Extended the per-column accumulator to carry an Option<Vec<bool>> validity (None = all-valid, the free common case; Some once an NA appears). A nullable numeric column now emits from_i64_values_with_validity / from_f64_values_with_validity directly. Bit-identical (PROBED + 605/0 + dump): Int64 missing -> Null(Null); Float64 missing -> Null(NaN) (matches build_csv_object_aware_column's Float normalize); int->null->float promotes to Float64; ALL-NA or 0-row column routes to Fallback -> Null dtype (a has_value guard — an all-null typed column would be the wrong dtype). Only bool/text still triggers full Fallback.

COLD 500k x 5 (4 nullable-numeric @10% null + 1 str), min-of-many, load ~19, pandas 2.2.3:
| fp before (Scalar fallback) | fp after (typed+validity) | pandas | ratio |
| ---: | ---: | ---: | ---: |
| 212.2ms | 156.5ms | 148.6ms | 0.70x -> 0.95x (near-parity) |
All-valid numeric path unchanged (adds only a None-check branch per cell; valid stays None). read_csv mixed now near-parity/win across all-valid AND nullable numeric shapes. CONFIRMED no regression on the all-valid path (re-measured quiet-box: numeric-heavy 172.5ms vs 177ms pre-nullable baseline — within noise, marginally faster).

### 2026-07-03 BlackThrush — read_json is a WIN (don't chase); read_csv 2-pass raw-avoidance REJECTED
Dug for a fresh IO lever after closing read_csv. TWO findings (both keep future turns off dead ends):

(1) read_json IS ALREADY A WIN. read_json_str uses the same Vec<Scalar> round-trip (column_from_json_values) that read_csv had, BUT pandas read_json is slow (unlike its C csv tokenizer) so fp wins anyway: 300k x 3 records COLD, fp 189.9ms vs pandas 293.7ms = 1.55x (measured even under box load ~60). Applying the read_csv typed-accumulator would amplify it (~189→~140ms) but it's gold-plating a WIN (>1.0x) — deprioritized. DON'T re-chase read_json as a loss.

(2) read_csv 2-pass raw-avoidance REJECTED (analyzed, not landed). The read_csv residual is that raw bytes are built for ALL columns though only Utf8-result (Fallback) columns consult them (~36% of parse per memory). A single pass CANNOT avoid raw for numeric columns because a column's type is unknown until the last row (it can drop to Fallback anytime, needing all prior verbatim raw). The 2-pass alternative (pass1 typed-classify no-raw; pass2 re-parse+raw only Fallback cols) REGRESSES text-heavy CSVs: an all-text frame does pass1 (typed-attempt every cell, all fail) THEN pass2 (re-tokenize + parse_scalar + raw every cell) = strictly MORE work than today's single pass. A "cheap classification pass" isn't cheap (deciding numeric-vs-text IS the parse). Lossy reconstruction of numeric-cell verbatim from the typed buffer is the verbatim blocker. So raw-for-all is inherent to the single-pass design; read_csv stays at its current near-parity/win. The mixed-CSV gap is effectively closed.

### 2026-07-03 BlackThrush — read_parquet typed Arrow conversion + single-batch — LOSS 0.15-0.20x -> 0.24-0.39x (1.6-1.9x)
Fresh find: read_parquet was ~0.2x pandas/pyarrow. TWO causes fixed. (1) Default reader batch size (1024 rows) turned a 1M-row file into ~1000 tiny RecordBatches, each converted to a DataFrame then concat_dataframes'd — the many-batch + concat overhead dominated. Set batch_size = file row count (clamped) -> one batch, no concat. (2) record_batch_to_dataframe converted each Arrow array cell-by-cell into a Vec<Scalar> (~32 B/elem) then Column::new re-scanned it. Added arrow_array_to_column_typed: Int8..64/UInt8..64 -> typed i64 column, Float32/64 -> f64, Boolean -> bool, all-valid Utf8/LargeUtf8 -> from_utf8_contiguous, all with Arrow null buffer -> ValidityMask direct; Date/Timestamp (chrono string coercion) + nullable-Utf8 keep the Scalar path. Bit-identical: VERIFIED vs pandas(pyarrow) on a 40-row parquet (nullable Int64->Null(Null), nullable Float64->Null(NaN), bool, str, int32) byte-for-byte; fp-io 605/0 (incl. parquet roundtrip).

COLD read, min-of-many, load ~15-18, pandas 2.2.3 + pyarrow 24:
| workload | fp before | fp after | pandas(pyarrow) | ratio |
| --- | ---: | ---: | ---: | ---: |
| read_parquet 1M x 4 mixed (int/float/bool/str) | 247.2ms | 128.1ms | 50.1ms | 0.20x -> 0.39x |
| read_parquet 1M x 6 numeric | 215.5ms | 137.6ms | 32.4ms | 0.15x -> 0.24x |
1.6-1.9x fp-side WIN, still sub-parity. RESIDUAL (documented-hard external floor): pyarrow's C++ parquet decoder + zero-copy Arrow->numpy views are genuinely faster than the Rust `parquet` crate decode + fp's owned-buffer column model (each column is memcpy'd out of the Arrow buffer; fp can't alias Arrow's Arc<Buffer>). The Scalar round-trip and the batch/concat overhead — the parts fp controlled — are now gone.

### 2026-07-03 BlackThrush — write_parquet is a WIN and ENCODE-bound (Scalar round-trip NOT the bottleneck; don't chase)
After landing the read_parquet fix (e143719a2), checked write_parquet as its sibling. FINDING: write_parquet already WINS — 1M x 6 numeric, fp write_parquet_bytes 128.5ms vs pandas to_parquet(BytesIO) 142.9ms = 1.11x. column_to_arrow_array DOES have the same per-cell values() Vec<Scalar> round-trip the read path had, so I prototyped a typed write (as_i64/f64_slice + Arrow builder append_slice/typed-loop, bit-identical, verified: 605/0 + fp-write→pandas-read roundtrip with nullable Int64/Float64/NaN/bool/str all correct incl. null positions). But clean same-load A/B: typed 122.7ms vs Scalar 126.7ms = ~3%, WITHIN NOISE. The parquet ENCODE (dictionary/RLE + snappy compression, ~120ms) dominates; the Scalar materialization is only ~4ms of it. REVERTED the typed write (not a real win). DON'T chase the write-side Scalar round-trip — write_parquet is encode-bound and already beats pandas. (Contrast the READ path, where the Scalar round-trip + tiny-batch concat WAS the bottleneck and the fix was a real 1.6-1.9x — see [[read-parquet-scalar-batch]].)

### 2026-07-03 BlackThrush — read_parquet CORRECTION (prior numbers were a bench artifact) + lazy index; MIXED is a WIN
Re-profiled read_parquet (decode-only = 27ms, FASTER than pyarrow's 32ms full read — the Rust parquet decode is NOT the floor). The ~110ms residual I attributed to "decode floor" in e143719a2 was actually TWO things: (a) the bench itself called df.column(..).values() to "force materialization", which BOXES the typed columns back to Vec<Scalar> (~60ms) — a cost pandas never pays and my e143719a2-fix INTRODUCED (typed columns box on .values(), Scalar-backed old columns don't), so the bench UNFAIRLY penalized the new path; (b) a small Vec<IndexLabel> materialization for the default 0..n index.

FIXED (b): record_batch_to_dataframe now uses Index::new_known_unique_int64_unit_range(0, n_rows) (lazy) instead of Index::new(Vec<IndexLabel> of n_rows). Fixed the bench to not box columns (df.index().len(), not per-cell .values()).

TRUE numbers (FAIR bench: read + O(1) index len, no Scalar boxing; the typed i64/f64 column buffers ARE the materialized data, like numpy), min-of-many, load ~13-17, pandas 2.2.3 + pyarrow 24:
| workload | original (pre-e143719a2) | now | pandas(pyarrow) | ratio |
| --- | ---: | ---: | ---: | ---: |
| read_parquet 1M x 4 mixed | 208ms | 45.7ms | 50ms | 0.24x -> 1.10x WIN |
| read_parquet 1M x 6 numeric | 116ms | 71.3ms | 32ms | 0.28x -> 0.45x |
So read_parquet mixed is now a WIN (not the 0.39x loss e143719a2 reported), and numeric is 0.45x (not 0.24x) — a 4.6x / 1.6x fp-side gain. LESSON: benching read_* by summing .values() boxes typed columns → penalizes typed-column readers; measure via a typed accessor or index len. Residual numeric gap = pyarrow zero-copy Arrow->numpy vs fp's owned-buffer copy (the memcpy out of Arrow's buffer, ~40ms for 6 cols) — the true, smaller floor. See [[read-parquet-scalar-batch]].

### 2026-07-03 BlackThrush — read_parquet all-valid numeric: from_*_values_owned (MOVE not copy) — 0.43x -> 0.52x
Continued the read_parquet numeric residual (pyarrow zero-copy floor). arrow_array_to_column_typed built the typed column via Column::from_i64_values/from_f64_values, which do Arc::from(Vec) = a second alloc+memcpy (~28ms/5M) ON TOP of the Arrow-buffer gather. Switched the all-valid arms to from_i64_values_owned/from_f64_values_owned, which MOVE the gathered Vec into the backing (one Arc::new, no copy — the [[f64-arc-copy-on-produce]] move-fix). Only the all-valid numeric arms change; nullable/bool/Utf8 paths byte-identical. fp-io 605/0.

FAIR bench (df.index().len(), no Scalar boxing), min-of-10, load ~12-13:
| workload | copy (from_*_values) | move (_owned) | pandas(pyarrow) | ratio |
| --- | ---: | ---: | ---: | ---: |
| read_parquet 1M x 6 numeric | 75.3ms | 62.1ms | 32ms | 0.43x -> 0.52x |
~17% fp-side. Mixed (2 numeric + bool + str) measured ~48→51ms build-to-build but that is measurement/build jitter, NOT a regression: the change only makes the 2 numeric columns cheaper (move<copy) and leaves bool/str code byte-identical, so mixed cannot slow from it. Residual numeric 0.52x = the last copy (Arrow buffer -> Vec gather, unavoidable in fp's owned-buffer model) vs pyarrow zero-copy Arrow->numpy. read_parquet mixed stays a WIN (~1.0-1.1x). See [[read-parquet-scalar-batch]].

### 2026-07-03 BlackThrush — merge_asof typed key extraction — LOSS 0.83x -> 1.44x WIN
merge_asof (ordered time-series join) extracted its `on` key columns via asof_numeric_values, which iterated column.values() (a per-cell Vec<Scalar> materialization) + to_f64() per element — for BOTH left and right keys. Added a typed fast path: an all-valid Int64/Float64 key reads the raw &[i64]/&[f64] slice directly (Int64 -> v as f64, Float64 -> v — exactly what to_f64() yields for those all-valid dtypes). Nullable / Timedelta64 / Datetime64 keys keep the Scalar loop. Bit-identical: VERIFIED vs pandas 2.2.3 on backward/forward/nearest directions (5x5, incl. the leading unmatched null-fill) EXACT; fp-join 138/0.

bench 1M left / 1M right, sorted i64 keys (left *3, right *2), backward, min-of-15, load ~15, pandas 2.2.3:
| op | before (Scalar keys) | after (typed keys) | vs pandas |
| --- | ---: | ---: | ---: |
| `merge_asof(backward)` 1M/1M | 19.1ms | 11.1ms | 0.83x -> 1.44x WIN (pandas 15.9ms) |
FLIPS LOSS->WIN. The 2x-1M-key Scalar materialization was ~8ms of the 19ms. Covers backward/forward/nearest (all via merge_asof_simple -> asof_numeric_values).

### 2026-07-03 BlackThrush — SURFACED: merge_asof converts keys to f64 (precision-lossy >2^53); Datetime64 rejected, large-i64/timedelta silently wrong
Probing datetime merge_asof (the primary time-series asof case) found fp REJECTS a Datetime64 `on` column ("must be numeric") — asof_numeric_values special-cases Timedelta64 but not Datetime64, so value.to_f64() errors. ROOT ISSUE (deeper): asof_numeric_values returns Vec<f64> and the whole asof matching (ensure_sorted_non_decreasing + the match scan) runs on f64. Datetime64 ns (~1.6e18) FAR exceeds f64's 2^53 integer precision, so a naive Datetime64->ns->f64 extraction COLLAPSES all keys to ~one value → wrong matches. VERIFIED the trap: an added Datetime64 arm made merge_asof RUN but return all-last-row (backward) / all-first-row (forward) instead of pandas' per-row matches — silently WRONG, strictly worse than the clean rejection. REVERTED it. Note the existing i64/Timedelta64 asof is ALSO silently wrong for keys > 2^53 (~104 days of ns), a latent pre-existing correctness bug — not just datetime. (The i64 typed-key WIN eb13347e5 is unaffected: it's bit-identical to the old f64 path, same precision behavior, just faster.)
CORRECT FIX (substantial, deferred): make the asof matching operate on i64 (ns) directly — an as_i64_slice/as_datetime64_slice/as_timedelta64_slice keyed path with i64 comparison (NAT/NaN as a separate mask), not a lossy f64 cast. Not landed: rewiring the asof match scan + sort-check + tolerance to i64 is a real change; a shift-by-min hack only works for <104-day ns ranges so it's unsafe (silent-wrong for multi-year data). Datetime asof stays REJECTED (a clean error beats silent-wrong) until the i64 path lands.

### 2026-07-03 BlackThrush — merge_asof Datetime64 keys NOW WORK (was REJECTED) via shift-to-f64
Follows the 2ada03a8f surface. fp merge_asof REJECTED Datetime64 `on` columns ("must be numeric") — the primary time-series asof case was unusable. FIX (try_asof_datetime_shift): when both keys are Datetime64, extract ns and SHIFT both sides by the global min so values fit f64's 2^53 precision exactly (shift is order- and difference-preserving, so the f64 asof match + tolerance stay exact). Gated: if the ns RANGE exceeds 2^53 (~104 days) it returns a CLEAN rejection (better than the silently-wrong result a lossy cast gives — see the surfaced trap). NaT/null -> NaN (no match). Non-Datetime keys unchanged.

CORRECTNESS: VERIFIED bit-identical vs pandas 2.2.3 on backward/forward/nearest (incl. leading unmatched NA + per-row matches) EXACT; long-range (>104-day) input rejects cleanly; fp-join 138/0. This is a COMPAT FIX (a rejected pandas feature now works), not a perf change.

perf (secondary — there was no "before", it errored), 1M/1M short-range datetime, load ~12:
| op | fp | pandas 2.2.3 |
| --- | ---: | ---: |
| `merge_asof(datetime, backward)` 1M/1M | 22.3ms | 15.5ms (0.70x) |
0.70x (the global-min scan + 2x shift add ~11ms over the i64 path), but the op went from REJECTED to CORRECT. Long-range datetime + the full i64-precision asof rewrite remain the deferred lever ([[join-scalar-materialization-vein]]). df.nlargest confirmed a 20x WIN (pandas nlargest slow) — not a lever.

### 2026-07-03 BlackThrush — merge_asof datetime shift all-valid fast path — 0.73x -> 0.89x
Follow-up to 5d1989abd (datetime asof shift-to-f64). try_asof_datetime_shift did a per-element ValidityMask::get bit-test in BOTH the min-scan and the shift. Added an all-valid fast path: when a side has no null bits (lv.all()/rv.all(), the hot case for an asof `on` key), skip the bit-test — only the cheap NaT sentinel compare remains (vectorizes). Bit-identical (asof_dt_verify bwd/fwd/nearest still EXACT vs pandas; fp-join 138/0).
Clean same-load A/B (load ~9), 1M/1M short-range datetime backward:
| | before | after | pandas |
| --- | ---: | ---: | ---: |
| merge_asof(datetime) 1M/1M | 21.3ms | 17.5ms | 15.5ms (0.73x -> 0.89x) |
~18% faster, near parity. df.corrwith (9.7x) + df.nlargest (20x) confirmed WINS this pass (pandas loops in Python) — not levers.

### 2026-07-03 BlackThrush — Datetime64 typed scattered gather in take_positions — LOSS 0.12x -> 0.35x (3x fp-side)
Column::take_positions (the gather behind Series.take / df.iloc / merge output / sort_values / loc on a datetime column) had typed Float64/Int64/Utf8 arms but NO Datetime64 arm — a Datetime64 column fell to the generic per-row Vec<Scalar::Datetime64> materialization (32 B/elem boxing + from_values rescan). Measured 2M scattered gather: datetime 146ms vs the i64 sibling 25ms (~5.8x slower) and vs pandas .take 17.3ms = 0.12x. Added a Datetime64 arm mirroring the Int64 gather (as_datetime64_slice -> gather ns by position -> from_datetime64_values). Bit-identical (Datetime64 stores i64 ns; from_datetime64_values materializes Scalar::Datetime64(ns) exactly as the primitive path, NaT sentinel carried through; verified + fp-columnar 467/0 + fp-frame 3109/0).
2M scattered take, min-of-8, pandas(numpy) 2.2.3:
| op | before (Scalar) | after (typed) | pandas | ratio |
| --- | ---: | ---: | ---: | ---: |
| datetime take 2M scattered | 146ms | 49ms | 17.3ms | 0.12x -> 0.35x |
3x fp-side, broad (all datetime-column gathers). RESIDUAL to reach ~parity (i64 is 15ms): lazy_all_valid_datetime64 does Arc::from(Vec) = a 16MB copy (TRIPLES memory traffic: gather-write + copy-read + copy-write) where the i64 path MOVES via lazy_all_valid_int64_owned. Needs a LazyAllValidDatetime64Vec owned variant (substantial — touches every ScalarValues match arm), deferred. Timedelta64 has the same gap (no from_timedelta64_values ctor).

### 2026-07-03 BlackThrush — SURFACED: reindex_by_positions (null-introducing gather) is Scalar for Datetime64/Timedelta64 — 0.12x
Sibling of the take_positions temporal fixes (34e4374b8/542631050/ed8c1a716). reindex_by_positions (the NULL-introducing gather behind OUTER joins, align, where/combine on differing indexes, Series/df.reindex) has typed Int64/Float64/Utf8 arms but NO Datetime64/Timedelta64 — a datetime/timedelta source falls to the per-row Scalar clone + Column::new. FIX (deferred — needs a NULLABLE typed variant, unlike take's all-valid case): add LazyNullableDatetime64 + LazyNullableTimedelta64 ScalarValues variants (Arc<Vec<i64>> + ValidityMask, mirror of lazy_nullable_int64/float64; missing -> Scalar::missing_for_dtype = Null(NaT), valid -> Datetime64/Timedelta64(ns) via ColumnData::to_scalars which ALREADY handles both) + from_{datetime,timedelta}64_values_with_validity + the two reindex_by_positions arms (gather ns, 0/NAT datum at missing, set validity bit for present). Exhaustive-match will flag the values/len/clone arms (compile-safe, ~3-4 sites each as the take variants did). NOT landed: a nullable temporal variant is a 3rd ScalarValues addition; deferred to keep this turn scoped. The take_positions (all-present / inner) temporal path is already fixed; this is the null-fill/outer path.

### 2026-07-03 BlackThrush — SeriesGroupBy WIDE/high-card i64 key CLOSED (0.41x -> 1.7-1.8x WIN)
The single biggest documented remaining loss. A SeriesGroupBy on a WIDE/high-cardinality i64 key (2M rows, ~490k groups: (splitmix%500000)*7919) bailed dense_group_ids' i64 arm (i64_dense_histogram_range fails: span 3.9e9 > 2^24) AND had no Utf8 arm, so ALL dense consumers fell to the generic build_groups tail — n Scalar materializations + FxHashMap<ScalarKey,Vec<usize>> with one heap Vec per group. Measured fp 315ms vs pandas 128-140ms = 0.41x.
FIX: added a wide-i64 open-addressing arm to dense_group_ids (splitmix64 probe, pow2 table ~0.67 load, inline i64 slots + u32 gid codes, i64::MIN empty-sentinel handled out-of-band) — the same primitive that beats khash on wide-i64 unique/isin/value_counts. First-seen gid order matches build_groups; dense_group_labels reads ks[i] at each g==order.len(), so bit-identical. ONE branch flips every dense consumer.
| op (2M rows, 490k grp) | fp before | fp after | pandas | ratio |
| SeriesGroupBy.sum wide-i64 | 315ms | 76ms | 139.6ms | 0.41x -> 1.84x |
| SeriesGroupBy.mean wide-i64 | ~315ms | 75ms | 128.7ms | -> 1.72x |
| SeriesGroupBy.max wide-i64 | ~315ms | 77ms | 133.3ms | -> 1.72x |
Correctness: per-group sums == grand sum (diff 0.000); fp-frame suite 3269/0. 4.15x fp-side. Supersedes the stale seriesgroupby-wide-i64-key-khash-floor 0.05x claim (that was a 500k-row all-unique baseline; the khash floor is BEATEN by the open-addr table here, as it was for the other wide-i64 hash ops). Also re-confirmed this session: df.sort_values shuffled-f64 2M = 114ms vs pandas 141ms = 1.24x WIN (the documented radix loss is now a win — don't re-probe).

### 2026-07-03 BlackThrush — SGB wide-i64 win: FRESH-vs-REUSED groupby caveat (honesty note on the entry above)
Re-measured pandas rigorously: the ratio hinges on whether pandas amortizes its factorize. pandas FRESH `s.groupby(key).sum()` re-factorizes every call = 164ms min / 237ms median → fp 76ms = 1.7-2.2x WIN (the common single-agg idiom `df.groupby('k')['v'].sum()`, correctly what the entry above claims). pandas REUSED object `g=s.groupby(key); g.sum()` caches the factorize = 26.8ms → fp 76ms = 0.35x LOSS, because fp's `SeriesGroupBy::sum()` recomputes dense_group_ids EVERY call (lazy borrow-struct, no gid cache). The stale memory "pandas 14.8ms" was this reused-object number. The landed win stands for fresh construction; RESIDUAL LEVER (open) = cache (gids,ngroups) on the SeriesGroupBy object so reused-multi-agg wins too. RULE: always state fresh-vs-reused in groupby benches — ~6x apart on high-card keys.

### 2026-07-03 BlackThrush — SeriesGroupBy reused-object factorize cache CLOSES the last groupby gap (0.35-0.73x -> 1.7-1.9x WIN)
Follow-up to the wide-i64 win above: fp's SeriesGroupBy recomputed dense_group_ids (the factorize) on EVERY agg call, so a reused groupby object (`g=s.groupby(k); g.sum(); g.mean(); g.max()`) re-factorized 3x while pandas caches its factorize in the GroupBy object. Added an interior OnceCell<Option<(Rc<[usize]>, usize)>> field to SeriesGroupBy: dense_group_ids() now computes once and hands out an O(1) Rc clone (gid Vec is n usizes = 16MB @2M, so Rc not Vec clone). Layout depends only on `by` (immutably borrowed) so the cache is never stale; fresh construction pays once, exactly as before. compute_dense_group_ids holds the old body; 28 consumers unchanged (Rc derefs to [usize]; only 3 for/zip sites needed .iter()).
| pattern (2M rows, 490k grp) | fp before | fp after | pandas | ratio |
| FRESH single gb.sum (new obj) | 76ms | 81ms | 121ms | 1.49x WIN (unchanged) |
| WARM single gb.sum (reused obj, cached) | 76ms | 14ms | 26.8ms | 0.35x -> 1.9x WIN |
| REUSED sum+mean+max (1 obj, 3 aggs) | ~228ms | 96ms | 166ms | 0.73x -> 1.73x WIN |
Bit-identical (cache is transparent — same gids, same output): fp-frame 3269/0. fp now WINS every groupby-agg pattern on a wide-i64 key: fresh, warm-single, reused-multi. This is the "residual lever" flagged in the prior entry, now landed.

### 2026-07-03 BlackThrush — wide-i64 groupby: transform breadth confirmed (40x-loss -> 14.7x WIN) + DFGB surface
Two confirmations after the SGB wide-i64 open-addr dense_group_ids arm (3415e3d8e) + factorize cache (d571def81):
1. SGB.transform on a wide-i64 key (2M rows, 490k grp) — memory had this at 0.025x (835ms, 40x slower). It routes through dense_group_ids' now-open-addr arm and the object cache warms across calls: transform(sum) 9.2ms vs pandas 135.8ms = **14.7x WIN**; transform(mean) 11.4ms vs 133.8ms = **11.7x WIN**. Bit-identical (fp-frame 3269/0). The two landed commits flip the ENTIRE SGB wide-i64 surface (sum/mean/max/count/transform), not just the reductions.
2. DataFrameGroupBy wide-i64 (same key) — SURFACED, no change landed. df.groupby.sum FRESH 56ms vs pandas 139.4ms = 2.49x WIN; mean 60ms vs 125ms = 2.08x WIN (DFGB's generic build_groups is already efficient; the i64_dense_histogram_range bail to generic is NOT a fresh-path loss here). REUSED-object df.groupby.sum: fp 56ms (no factorize cache) vs pandas 16.3ms = 0.29x. A DFGB factorize cache (mirror of the SGB one) would need a Rc-return ripple across 12 int64_dense_grouping call sites + the shared utf8 grouping return type (they unify into one tuple type) on the 78k-line hot lib.rs — and even zero-copy the best case is ~parity (fold ~15ms vs pandas' fast 16ms), NOT a real win. NOT worth the invasive refactor: DFGB fresh already wins, reused caps at parity. Don't chase DFGB reused caching.

### 2026-07-03 BlackThrush — Series.between nullable-f64 SIMD vectorization (0.16x->0.25x; all-valid confirmed 1.44x WIN)
Probing elementwise ops found Series.between the one loss: on a 10%-NaN Float64 (2M) it was 8.17ms vs pandas 1.32ms = 0.16x. The typed all-valid path (as_f64_slice) already vectorizes and WINS, but a NaN-bearing column bails it to the nullable path whose per-element `valid.get(i)` bitmask read inside the map closure blocked SIMD. Rewrote it to run the predicate over the raw f64 slice in a hoisted per-mode loop (AUTO-VECTORIZES, identical to the all-valid arm) then force missing slots to false via `for_each_invalid_range` (no-op when all-valid). Bit-identical (fp-frame 3269/0): valid⇒predicate, missing⇒false.
| between f64 2M | fp before | fp after | pandas | ratio |
| ALL-VALID (no NaN) | 0.64ms | 0.64ms | 0.92ms | 1.44x WIN (pre-existing) |
| 10%-NaN | 8.17ms | 5.33ms | 1.32ms | 0.16x -> 0.25x |
The NaN case stays sub-parity: RESIDUAL is STRUCTURAL — pandas gets NaN handling for FREE (NaN compares false in native SIMD, no validity concept), while fp must run a SEPARATE validity-fixup pass because `from_f64_values_with_validity` stores data as-is (a masked slot may hold a non-NaN in-range value, so the fixup can't be skipped). The fixup cost scales with NaN fragmentation (worst-case at 10% random NaN; contiguous null runs are near-free). Same family as the f64-arc-copy structural tax. Landed the bit-identical 1.53x fp-side improvement; all-valid between (the common case) WINS. Other elementwise ops re-confirmed WINS this probe: interpolate 10.4x, map(i64 dict) 3.3x, clip 2.1x; str.contains/replace/upper/len/startswith/slice 4.4-11x; groupby std/var wide-i64 SGB 6.1x / DFGB.std 1.61x.

### 2026-07-03 BlackThrush — 3-key (K>=3) bounded-Int64 merge INNER/LEFT CLOSED (0.43-0.57x -> 1.1-1.6x WIN)
Probing found merge on 3 Int64 keys a real loss: it hit typed_wide_multi_i64_key_positions, which builds K separate FxHashMap<i64,u32> factorizations over left∪right (the khash floor) then u128-packs. 500kx500k, each key %500 (~490k out rows): INNER 194.5ms vs pandas 110.0ms = 0.57x; LEFT 257.9ms vs 109.7ms = 0.43x.
FIX: added bounded_composite_multi_i64_key_positions — for all-valid Int64 keys whose joint mixed-radix span fits i64 (Σ_c (val_c-min_c)·stride_c, stride_0=1, a bijection on the key box), pack each row's composite into ONE i64 and run the SINGLE-key i64 hash merge (hash_i64_inner_positions_slices / mirror of hash_int64_left_positions). Skips all K factorization maps — just K min/max scans + arithmetic. Dispatched before the factorize path; bails (->factorize) on nullable keys, span overflow, or Outer (Outer's key-ascending emit stays on the factorize path's semantic gid sort).
| merge 3-key i64 500kx500k | fp before | fp after | pandas | ratio |
| INNER | 194.5ms | 68.0ms | 110.0ms | 0.57x -> 1.62x WIN |
| LEFT | 257.9ms | 100.8ms | 109.7ms | 0.43x -> 1.09x WIN |
Verified: fp-join 143/0; order-independent checksum differential vs pandas MATCHES exactly for both inner (5410 rows) and left (5411 rows) — same pairs + same unmatched-left null-fill. Bit-identical to the factorize path (structural mirror: same left-order probe, ascending right buckets). Wide/unbounded 3-key keys (span > i64) correctly stay on the factorize path. RESIDUAL: Outer 3-key still on factorize (khash floor); >i64-span keys unchanged. Also re-confirmed WINS this probe: merge LEFT single-i64 2.51x, RIGHT single-i64 1.20x (the documented 0.96x RIGHT parity is closed); dt.* accessors 6-15x; interpolate 10.4x.

### 2026-07-03 BlackThrush — two SURFACED non-flippable results (probed, not landed)
1. 3-KEY OUTER merge composite-pack — reaches 0.81x, NOT a win, REVERTED. Extended bounded_composite_multi_i64_key_positions to Outer (col-0-most-significant strides so ascending-i64-composite == ascending (k0,k1,..) tuple, matching the factorize path's gid_tuple sort). 3-key OUTER 500kx500k: 300ms -> 236ms vs pandas 190.75ms = 0.64x -> 0.81x (bit-identical, checksum would match). But the composite-outer reduces to EXACTLY the existing hash_int64_outer_positions machinery (gid map + CSR + sort over ~1M distinct keys) — that IS the floor, and it matches pandas' own optimized outer cost. Skipping the K factorize maps only recovered ~64ms; the CSR+sort dominates and doesn't beat pandas. Reverted (0.81x is a non-win, and outer has no all-valid-win headline). Don't re-attempt composite-outer — the floor is the CSR+sort, not the factorize. Inner/left composite stay WINS (shipped 3ba0f3d93).
2. Series.round(2) single-col f64 — 0.60x, TARGET-LIMITED (not fixable without a build-target change). 1M f64: fp 1.20ms vs pandas 0.72ms. Root cause: round_ties_even_fast uses a magic-number ((|x|+2^52)-2^52) SSE2 trick because the f64::round_ties_even() intrinsic lowers to a per-element libm roundeven CALL on the BASELINE x86-64 target (no SSE4.1 roundpd) — a prior swap REGRESSED round 3.4->11.9ms. numpy gets 0.72ms via runtime CPU dispatch (AVX/roundpd). So the 1.67x gap is the baseline-target-vs-runtime-dispatch delta, not a code defect; and round has golden bit-identity tests (touching the kernel is golden-regen risk). df.round(2) 1Mx5 WINS 7.3x (fp 3.37ms vs pandas 24.52ms) — the common multi-col case dominates. Don't chase single-col round without a target-cpu bump. Also this probe: df.round 7.3x WIN confirms the stale "df_round 0.84x OPEN" memory is CLOSED.

### 2026-07-03 BlackThrush — Series.sum/mean f64 blocked (8-lane) summation — 0.41x/0.82x -> ~2.2x/4.2x WIN (+ MORE accurate)
Probing bandwidth reductions found Series.sum the biggest non-groupby loss: all-valid Float64 sum used `data.iter().sum()` — a 0.0-seeded sequential left-fold LLVM cannot auto-vectorize (f64 add non-associative) — 1.418ms vs pandas 0.583ms = 0.41x; mean (shared f64_valid_sum_count bit-scan) 1.453ms vs 1.189ms = 0.82x.
FIX: blocked_sum_f64 — 8 independent accumulator lanes over chunks_exact(8) + pairwise lane-combine + remainder fold. Wired into the all-valid sum path and f64_valid_sum_count's `validity.all()` fast path (feeds mean/var/std/describe).
| op (2M f64) | fp before | fp after | pandas | ratio |
| Series.sum | 1.418ms | 0.24ms | 0.580ms | 0.41x -> 2.2x WIN |
| Series.mean | 1.453ms | 0.27ms | 1.143ms | 0.82x -> 4.2x WIN |
NUMERICS (honest — NOT bit-identical to the old left-fold on large arrays, but VERIFIED safe + MORE accurate): for data.len()<8 the chunks_exact(8) iterator is empty so the whole slice flows through the remainder left-fold => BIT-IDENTICAL to iter().sum() on short arrays (every reduction conformance fixture is tiny) => fp-frame 3269/0 unchanged. On the 2M array: fp blocked = 14280792051.857233 vs numpy pairwise .857141 vs old-leftfold .857920 — the blocked result is ~8x CLOSER to numpy (pandas' own reference algorithm) than the left-fold was, and mean now matches numpy to 9 dp (7140.396025929). So the change tracks pandas' value MORE tightly while winning 2-4x. Pandas/numpy themselves sum pairwise (not left-fold), so no single fold is "the" bit-exact answer. Also this probe: max 1.93x, std 2.62x, cumsum 3.56x, pct_change 3.24x WINS; diff 0.87x is bandwidth-bound parity (32MB traffic, not winnable); melt 2.82x, pivot 1.98x WINS.

### 2026-07-03 BlackThrush — Series.dot typed blocked path — 0.37x -> 1.45x WIN (128MB Scalar-mat killed)
Applying the blocked-sum lever to more reductions found Series.dot the loss: it called `.values()` on BOTH columns (materializing 2·n boxed Scalars = 128MB @2M) then left-folded `result += x*y` with per-element to_f64() — 19.2ms vs pandas 7.16ms = 0.37x.
FIX: typed all-valid Float64 fast path. Same-index ⇒ blocked_dot_f64 (8-lane fma, sibling of blocked_sum_f64); same-labels-different-order ⇒ typed position-gather left-fold (bit-identical, no Scalar boxing). All-valid ⇒ the generic is_missing()->NaN guard never fires.
| Series.dot 2M f64 | before | after | pandas | ratio |
| same-index | 19.2ms | 4.93ms | 7.16ms | 0.37x -> 1.45x WIN |
Verified: fp-frame 3269/0; fp dot = 64898976556933.515625 vs numpy 64898976556933.742188 (rel ~3.5e-15, dot is order-dependent — blocked degenerates to left-fold for len<8 so conformance fixtures are bit-exact). Also this probe (all WINS, blocked-sum lever propagated): Series.var 4.33x / std 4.71x (now use blocked f64_valid_sum_count), df.sum(axis=0) 3.77x, df.mean(axis=0) 6.80x, Series.prod 2.25x. sum/mean/dot were the only non-groupby reduction losses; all CLOSED.

### 2026-07-03 BlackThrush — scalar comparison hoist match-out-of-closure — 4.3x fp-side (0.10x->0.42x); residual is SSE2-vs-AVX2 target gap
Series.eq/ne/gt/ge/lt/le _scalar (Column::compare_scalar) had `match op` INSIDE the per-element map closure. Even though `op` is loop-invariant, the branch was not hoisted → the compare didn't vectorize → scalar eq f64 2M = 3.345ms vs pandas 0.327ms = 0.10x. (Memory series-comparison-vein's "compare_scalar ~1.0x already unswitched" was a stale/phantom read.)
FIX: hoist the `match op` OUT of the closure into 6 monomorphic `v <op> s` loops (all three typed arms: all-valid f64, i64, nullable-f64). Bit-identical (pure reorganization — same per-op comparison). fp-columnar 467/0, fp-frame 3269/0.
| scalar compare 2M | before | after | pandas | ratio |
| eq f64 | 3.345ms | 0.78ms | 0.327ms | 0.10x -> 0.42x |
| gt f64 | ~3.3ms | 0.78ms | 0.398ms | -> 0.51x |
| gt i64 | ~3.3ms | 1.05ms | 0.371ms | -> 0.35x |
4.3x fp-side (enables SSE2 vectorization). NOT a win vs pandas: RESIDUAL is the SSE2-baseline-vs-AVX2 gap — numpy runtime-dispatches AVX2 (2x compare/pack width) while fp builds baseline x86-64 per a DELIBERATE .cargo/config decision. CRUCIAL: that decision (br-frankenpandas-jawxr, 2026-06-13) reverted +avx2 because it was NEUTRAL for the corr/cov/spearman GRAM (compute-bound, not FP-throughput-limited) — it did NOT test bandwidth-bound elementwise ops. HIGH-LEVERAGE META-SURFACE: an AVX2 build (or per-fn #[target_feature]) would likely close scalar-compare AND single-col round (0.60x, same SSE2 root, see prior entry) AND other elementwise bandwidth ops AT ONCE — the target-cpu decision deserves re-evaluation SPECIFICALLY for elementwise ops, separate from the Gram. Landed the hoist (strictly-better bit-identical code); the AVX gap is a build-policy lever, not a code defect.

### 2026-07-03 BlackThrush — BLOCKER SURFACED: elementwise-AVX class needs unsafe (workspace #![forbid(unsafe_code)]) or target-cpu bump
The remaining accessible losses are all one class: bandwidth-bound elementwise ops where numpy runtime-dispatches AVX2 and fp runs SSE2-baseline. Measured (2M, min-of-30, quiet): scalar compare eq f64 0.78ms vs pandas 0.327ms = 0.42x, gt f64 0.51x, gt i64 0.35x; single-col round(2) 0.60x (prior entry). Each ~2-2.4x = exactly the SSE2(2-wide)-vs-AVX2(4-wide) f64 ratio.
RADICAL LEVER ATTEMPTED (numpy's exact model, keeps the SSE2 baseline build): per-fn `#[target_feature(enable="avx2")]` AVX2 build of the compare loop + `is_x86_feature_detected!("avx2")` runtime dispatch + SSE2 fallback. Body is bit-identical (same IEEE f64 compares; only codegen target features differ).
HARD BLOCKER (verified — the dispatch was implemented and FAILED to compile): every fp crate (all 6: frame/columnar/index/join/io/types) has `#![forbid(unsafe_code)]` at line 1 — a uniform workspace invariant with ZERO existing unsafe. `#[target_feature]` + the `unsafe` call site are rejected: "error: implementation of an `unsafe` method / usage of an `unsafe` block" under forbid. `forbid` cannot be locally `#[allow]`-overridden; on stable Rust there is no safe SIMD (std::simd is nightly). Reverted the attempt (fp-columnar back to the committed hoist-only state — that hoist, 2b345d0c7, already landed the SSE2 vectorization 3.345->0.78ms).
TWO MAINTAINER FIXES (either closes compare + round + the whole elementwise-bandwidth class, each ~2x, flipping ~0.4x->~1.0x): (a) downgrade fp-columnar `#![forbid(unsafe_code)]`->`#![deny(unsafe_code)]` + a narrow `#[allow(unsafe_code)]` audited+tested AVX2 kernel module with runtime dispatch — AGENTS.md §56 explicitly permits "narrow unsafe usage ... isolate behind audited interfaces and tests", and this is exactly that (unavoidable: no safe stable SIMD; narrow: one kernel; guarded: runtime feature check + SSE2 fallback; bit-identical: verifiable by differential). (b) bump target-cpu to x86-64-v3 in .cargo/config — simpler but whole-binary; NOTE the prior +avx2 revert (br-frankenpandas-jawxr) only benchmarked the compute-bound corr/cov GRAM (where AVX was neutral), NOT bandwidth-bound elementwise ops, so that rejection does not apply to this class. Needs a maintainer decision (breaks a uniform 6-crate safety invariant / global build policy — not a unilateral perf-pass change).
SCOPE: everything else algorithmic is WON this session — all reductions (sum/mean/dot fixed + var/std/prod/df-axis0/df-axis1 6-15x), moments (skew 14.6x/kurt 15.3x/median 6.2x/sem 1.76x), reshape (melt 2.82x/pivot 1.98x), str (4-11x), dt (6-15x), groupby (fully), cum ops (2-4x), 3-key inner/left merge (1.1-1.6x). The elementwise-AVX class is the ONLY remaining non-structural gap and is policy-blocked.

### 2026-07-03 BlackThrush — probe sweep: regex/moments/axis1 WIN; binary-arith probable AVX+parity residual (load-contaminated)
Reliable WINS this probe (lower load): regex str.extract 3.1x / count 4.5x / replace 2.05x / contains 1088x; Series.skew 14.6x / kurt 15.3x / median 6.2x / sem 1.76x; df axis=1 sum 6.3x / mean 5.4x / max 10.9x. Everything algorithmic remains dominated.
BINARY ARITHMETIC (a+b/a*b/a/b, same-index 2M f64) — PROBABLE residual 0.85x/0.66x/0.65x vs pandas, but UNCONFIRMED: measured at load 45-79 (box overloaded — pandas baselines swung 2-2.5x across runs, so the ratio is unreliable; cf. the earlier compare-eq 1.471->0.327ms load phantom). NOT a clean lever: the same-index path (Column::aligned_binary_f64_same_positions) is already heavily optimized — parallel (threads at len>=1M) + fused single-sweep NaN-tracking + owned move, 3 prior bead iterations (f64simd/9houf/d3mfh). The residual splits into (a) the input-NaN scan, a PARITY cost numpy skips (a valid-NaN input must route to Scalar::Null(NaN), not Float64(NaN); locked by aligned_binary_f64_same_positions_matches_general_path test) and (b) the SSE2-vs-AVX2 gap (mul/div compute-heavier → worse ratio than add) — folds into the already-surfaced elementwise-AVX blocker. Tested raising PARALLEL_MIN_LEN (single-thread at 2M, per the "thread-spawn hurts L3-resident" memory) but load-79 made it inconclusive; reverted. Needs a QUIET box to confirm whether it's a real loss or a load phantom before spending a lever on already-3x-iterated code. No src change landed.

### 2026-07-03 BlackThrush — LOCATED lever (quiet-box): bandwidth-op parallel thresholds sit below the df_abs 4M floor
Load sustained 68-90 across 4 turns blocks measured benching, so this is a LOCATED-not-landed finding (a threshold change is unverifiable + could regress other sizes/machines without a clean A/B). Concrete target for a quiet box:
- The df-abs-bandwidth memory ESTABLISHED (measured: df_abs 0.25x->7.5x) that thread-spawn HURTS L3-resident bandwidth ops and set a ~4M serial floor. But two hot bandwidth paths parallelize far below that:
  - `apply_f64_slices_nan_tracked` (crates/fp-columnar/src/lib.rs:1618) — `PARALLEL_MIN_LEN = 1<<20` (1M). Drives same-index Series a+b/a*b/a/b (the probed 0.65-0.85x candidate).
  - `par_map_vec_f64` (crates/fp-columnar/src/lib.rs:6242) — `PAR_MIN = 200_000` (200k). Drives abs/clip/round/unary-f64.
- At 2M f64 (16MB, L3-resident) both spawn ~8 threads per op; under load that's catastrophic, and even quiet the df_abs lesson says it's a net loss until data exceeds L3 (~4M elems / 32MB, and MORE for the 3-buffer arith a,b,out=48MB).
- FIX (quiet box): raise both thresholds toward the ~4M-element floor (tune per the 2-buffer vs 3-buffer traffic), A/B parallel-vs-serial INTERLEAVED under identical load (load-robust ratio), across 1M/2M/4M/16M/64M to confirm serial wins in-L3 and parallel wins beyond. Bit-identical either way (threshold only gates parallelization, not results) so conformance stays green by construction; the win is the ratio, which needs the quiet box. Do NOT change blind — the optimal floor is machine-L3-specific.
This supersedes the generic "needs quiet box" note on the binary-arith entry with the exact code sites + the established principle to apply.

### 2026-07-03 BlackThrush — CORRECTION: bandwidth-op parallel threshold is NOT too low — DEBUNKED by interleaved A/B (parallel wins at all sizes)
The prior "LOCATE bandwidth-op parallel thresholds below the df_abs 4M floor" entry was WRONG — retracted. Executed the load-robust method it prescribed (temporary ZZ_FORCE_SERIAL_ARITH atomic toggle in apply_f64_slices_nan_tracked → measure parallel vs serial INTERLEAVED in one process, identical per-iteration load, min-of-25×4-rounds, load 14). Result: PARALLEL WINS at EVERY size for the real Series a+b:
| n | par | serial | serial/par |
| 2M | 1.973ms | 2.389ms | 1.21x PAR WINS |
| 4M | 4.251ms | 4.773ms | 1.12x PAR WINS |
| 8M | 12.716ms | 37.587ms | 2.96x PAR WINS |
| 16M | 25.547ms | 74.120ms | 2.90x PAR WINS |
So PARALLEL_MIN_LEN=1<<20 is FINE (parallel already helps at 2M); raising it would REGRESS 2M-8M. The misleading earlier signals were artifacts: (1) a standalone microbench used a SIMPLER kernel (plain add, no NaN-tracking/alloc) with a different crossover; (2) separate parallel-vs-serial BUILDS were measured under DIFFERENT load windows (load swung 15-90), not interleaved. The real 1M->2M "super-linear" jump (0.559->2.072ms) is the L3->DRAM transition (1M: a+b+out=24MB ~L3-resident; 2M: 48MB > L3 => memory-bound), INHERENT and present in pandas too — NOT a parallelism penalty. Toggle reverted; fp-columnar clean. LESSON: for parallel-vs-serial threshold questions, ONLY trust an in-process INTERLEAVED A/B (runtime toggle) under identical load — separate builds + microbenches lie. Binary-arith residual (0.54-0.89x vs pandas) is purely the AVX gap + parity NaN-scan (already surfaced), NOT parallelism. This closes the arith investigation: no threshold lever exists.

### 2026-07-03 BlackThrush — reindex Int64 positions: u32 direct-address table (16MB->8MB) — 1.32x fp-side (0.58x->0.76x)
Probed reindex (memory had it "DECLINED"): Series.reindex on a wide/shuffled unique Int64 index (1M, source i*2 range 2M) = 55.5ms vs pandas 31ms = 0.56x. Split-timed: gather (reindex_by_positions, already typed) only 7.9ms, Index::new 2.4ms — the POSITIONS resolver (reindex_positions_int64_direct) is ~45ms, dominated by cache-random build+probe of a `vec![usize::MAX; range]` direct-address table (2M slots x 8B = 16MB > L2/L3).
FIX: table `usize`->`u32` (positions < src.len(), gated src.len() <= u32::MAX). Halves the table to 8MB, so the cache-random build AND probe touch a 2x smaller working set. Bit-identical (same first-occurrence positions, same absent->None; u32::MAX sentinel). fp-frame 3269/0.
| reindex shuffled-i64 1M | before | after | pandas | ratio |
| FULL | 55.5ms | 40.6ms | 31ms | 0.56x -> 0.76x |
1.32x fp-side, CONFIRMED robust: NEW measured 40.6ms even at load 30 beat OLD 53.7ms at load 17 (faster despite worse load — not a load phantom). NOT a win vs pandas: residual is the cache-random-access floor (8MB table still > L2) + the 24-byte IndexLabel enum scan (vs pandas' raw i64 khash) + khash's tighter packing. Further levers (sort-merge to convert random->sequential like the searchsorted finger-search win; raw-i64 view for lazy-backed indexes) are bigger rewrites with uncertain gain on a memory-DECLINED called-once op — deferred. Landed the low-risk provable cache win. Also this probe (all WINS): rank(dense) 4.3x/rank(first) 2.5x, drop_duplicates 1.4x, df.corr 10.4x, corr_spearman 1.94x, to_datetime 5.06x.

### 2026-07-03 BlackThrush — unaligned binop (differing UNSORTED Int64 indexes) CLOSED — 0.13x -> 1.41x WIN (11x fp-side)
The biggest gap found in many turns. Series a+b/a*b on two DIFFERING SHUFFLED (unsorted, unique) Int64 indexes (1M each, overlapping) = 845ms vs pandas 108.8ms = 0.13x (7.7x SLOWER). Root: the unaligned path's align_union_sorted_unique, for UNSORTED indexes, builds TWO std HashMap<IndexLabel,usize> (SipHash over 24-byte enum keys) AND inserts 2n labels into a cache-hostile BTreeSet<IndexLabel> (tree-of-boxed-nodes, slow enum cmp). The memory df-unaligned fix (align_union_sorted_plan two-cursor merge) only covered SORTED-differing indexes (align_union_merge_sorted); UNSORTED fell to this BTreeSet+SipHash fallback — for BOTH Series and DataFrame binops.
FIX: align_union_sorted_unique_i64_direct — typed direct-address union for unsorted unique bounded-Int64 indexes (mirrors the reindex u32-table lever). Two range-sized u32 tables record each value's only position per side; iterate the span ascending → sorted union + aligned positions in one pass. No hashing, no tree, no Scalar. Routed before the BTreeSet fallback; bails (→fallback) if either index isn't i64-viewable or the span exceeds the direct-address cap.
| unaligned binop 1M+1M shuffled-i64 | before | after | pandas | ratio |
| a+b | 845ms | 77.0ms | 108.8ms | 0.13x -> 1.41x WIN |
| a*b | 841ms | 77.8ms | 112.1ms | 0.13x -> 1.44x WIN |
11x fp-side. Bit-identical: fp-frame 3269/0 AND an order-sensitive checksum vs pandas MATCHES exactly (len 12000, ck 56671257529114025) on value=f(label) data (BTreeSet<IndexLabel::Int64> ascending == span-ascending; unique index => only position == first). Also fixes DataFrame unaligned binops on unsorted-Int64 indexes (shared align_union_sorted_unique). Confirmed robust (845->77 is 11x, far beyond load noise; load 14).

### 2026-07-03 BlackThrush — unaligned binop DATETIME64 + wide-Int64 indexes CLOSED — 0.30x -> 1.92x WIN (sort-merge)
Follow-up to the Int64-direct-address unaligned-binop fix: that fix is Int64-and-bounded only (int64_view rejects Datetime64; ns spans blow the 2^24 direct-address cap). So a+b on differing shuffled DATETIME64 indexes (1M, the ubiquitous time-series case) still hit the BTreeSet+SipHash fallback = 836.6ms vs pandas 248.2ms = 0.30x.
FIX: align_union_sorted_unique_i64_sortmerge — sort each side's (i64_value, position) pairs + two-cursor merge into the ascending union + aligned positions. Handles ANY i64 span, so it covers both Datetime64 ns AND WIDE Int64 (which the direct-address bails to fallback). i64 extraction via int64_view (Int64) or raw IndexLabel::Datetime64 ns; mixed kinds bail (cross-kind labels never match). Routed after the direct-address path, before the BTreeSet fallback.
| unaligned a+b DIFFERING 1M | before | after | pandas | ratio |
| Datetime64 index | 836.6ms | 129.2ms | 248.2ms | 0.30x -> 1.92x WIN |
6.5x fp-side. Bit-identical: fp-frame 3269/0 AND a datetime order-sensitive checksum vs pandas MATCHES exactly (len 9000, ck 28959335950457046) — Datetime64 ns sort ascending == IndexLabel::Datetime64, unique index => only position. Together with the Int64-direct-address fix, unaligned Series/DataFrame binops on differing Int64 (bounded+wide) AND Datetime64 indexes are ALL now typed (0.13-0.30x -> 1.4-1.9x); only Utf8/mixed indexes keep the BTreeSet fallback. This probe also: combine_first differing-idx 1.30x WIN, df1+df2 differing-idx 1.03x (parity, rescued from ~0.14x by the shared align fix); Series comparison on differing indexes N/A (pandas raises ValueError).

### 2026-07-03 BlackThrush — unaligned binop UTF8 indexes CLOSED — 0.79x -> 3.44x WIN (str sort-merge); alignment vein COMPLETE
Last uncovered align case: a+b on differing shuffled Utf8 indexes (500k) = 960ms vs pandas 756ms = 0.79x (pandas string-align is also slow). Same BTreeSet<IndexLabel::Utf8>(cache-hostile tree of Strings) + 2x SipHash HashMap fallback.
FIX: align_union_sorted_unique_utf8_sortmerge — extract each side's (&str, position) (zero-copy borrow of the already-allocated label Strings), sort by str, two-cursor merge → ascending union + aligned positions. Replaces the tree+hash with two flat sorts + a linear merge. Routed after the i64 sort-merge, before the fallback; bails on any non-Utf8 label.
| unaligned a+b DIFFERING 500k | before | after | pandas | ratio |
| Utf8 index | 960ms | 219.8ms | 756ms | 0.79x -> 3.44x WIN |
4.4x fp-side. Bit-identical: fp-frame 3269/0 AND a Utf8 order-sensitive checksum vs pandas MATCHES exactly (len 7500, ck 18514391254553895) — IndexLabel::Utf8 Ord == str lexicographic == BTreeSet order, unique index => one position.
ALIGNMENT VEIN COMPLETE (3 turns): unaligned Series/DataFrame binops on differing indexes are now ALL typed — Int64 bounded (direct-address, 1.41x) + Int64 wide/Datetime64 (i64 sort-merge, 1.92x) + Utf8 (str sort-merge, 3.44x). Only MIXED-kind indexes keep the BTreeSet fallback (rare; cross-kind labels never match anyway, so it's correctness-not-perf). The catastrophic 0.13-0.79x unaligned-binop class is closed.

### 2026-07-04 BlackThrush — reindex Datetime64/wide-Int64 = 0.14x; open-addr hash resolver REGRESSED (reverted); structural
Probed reindex on a shuffled DATETIME64 index (1M) = 288ms warm / 402ms COLD vs pandas 41.7ms = 0.14x. Cause: reindex_positions_int64_direct is bounded-Int64 only (matches IndexLabel::Int64, bounded span), so Datetime64/wide-Int64 fall to position_map_first's FxHashMap<IndexLabel> (24-byte enum keys).
ATTEMPTED (reverted — REGRESSED): reindex_positions_i64_hash — open-addressing i64 table (splitmix probe) of source ns->position, probe targets, handling Int64+Datetime64 via labels_i64_kind. Result: 699ms COLD single-call — WORSE than the 402ms FxHashMap it replaced. Two reasons: (1) extracting a Vec<i64> from 2n IndexLabel enums (24B each, 48MB scan) before hashing; (2) the pow2 open-addr table (~2M slots x 12B = 24MB) is cache-random on both build and probe and lost to FxHash. Tree reverted clean.
STRUCTURAL: pandas' 41.7ms is a raw-int64 khash — fp pays a 24-byte-IndexLabel-enum->i64 extraction pandas never does (the index stores IndexLabel, not a raw ns Arc<[i64]>). A sort-merge (like the align vein win) would be sequential for the sort but still needs a cache-random SCATTER-back to target order AND the same extraction, so best-case ~2x fp-side (~200ms) = still ~0.20x — sub-parity. Truly closing it needs a raw-ns (Arc<[i64]>) view on Datetime64 indexes (structural, mirrors int64_view for Int64) so the resolver skips the enum extraction — a deferred fp-index lever. reindex is memory-DECLINED (called-once, alloc-bound); NOT worth the open-addr/sort-merge complexity for a sub-parity result. Don't retry open-addr hash. LESSON: the align sort-merge won because it replaced a BTreeSet (far worse than a hash); reindex already uses a hash (FxHashMap), so hashing differently doesn't help — the win would need to eliminate the IndexLabel extraction itself.

### 2026-07-04 BlackThrush — CORRECTION + CLOSED: reindex Datetime64/wide-Int64 was NOT structural — route Series.reindex fallback through get_indexer (37x fp-side WARM)
The prior "reindex ... = 0.14x ... structural, sub-parity" entry was WRONG on the mechanism and the verdict — retracted. Two errors: (1) it claimed the fallback used `FxHashMap<IndexLabel>`; it actually used `Index::position_map_first()` = std `HashMap<IndexLabel>` (SipHash over 24-byte enum keys), REBUILT every call and never cached. (2) It missed that `Index::get_indexer` ALREADY routes unsorted-unique Datetime64/wide-Int64/Utf8 through the typed, identity-cached resolvers (`unique_datetime64_positions`/`unsorted_unique_int64_positions`/`unique_utf8_positions` — `FxHashMap<i64>`/`<&str>`, 8-byte/borrowed keys, cached by the source index's `label_identity`, the same machinery that made get_indexer 4.1x WIN). Series.reindex (fp-frame) just hand-rolled `position_map_first` instead of calling get_indexer, so it never reached that fast path.
FIX (one line): `Series::reindex` non-bounded-Int64 fallback `position_map_first()`-probe → `self.index.get_indexer(&new_index)`. Bit-identical: duplicates rejected above ⇒ self unique ⇒ get_indexer first-occurrence == only-occurrence == old `HashMap::get`; IndexLabel Eq kind-sensitive ⇒ absent/other-kind → None as before. (`reindex_positions_int64_direct` bounded-Int64 fast path unchanged, tried first.)
Measured A/B (same box, same session, INTERLEAVED build/run; box loaded so absolutes are high but the ratio is load-robust — 16-37x far beyond any load noise):
| reindex shuffled 1M | BEFORE | AFTER | fp-side |
| Datetime64 WARM (reused source) | 1343.6ms | 36.0ms | 37x |
| wide-Int64 WARM | 1560.3ms | 58.4ms | 27x |
| Datetime64 COLD (fresh source/call) | 2253.5ms | 137.8ms | 16x |
vs pandas quiet-box 41.7ms: WARM 36.0ms = 0.14x -> **1.16x WIN** (the repeated-alignment-loop pattern — pandas caches its index engine identically, so warm-vs-warm is the fair compare and fp now wins). COLD single-call 137.8ms = 0.14x -> ~0.30x (still sub-parity: one cold FxHashMap<i64> build + the IndexLabel->i64 extraction pandas skips — the residual the prior entry correctly identified, but 16x smaller now). fp-frame 3106/0 on the reindex path (the 3 acosh/arccosh golden fails are the known 1-ULP remote-worker libm flake, unrelated — trig, zero overlap with reindex). LESSON: before declaring a resolver "structural", check whether a SIBLING op (here get_indexer) already solved the same lookup — the gap may be a mis-routed call, not a missing algorithm. The open-addr attempt failed because it replaced the wrong thing; the real fix reused existing cached machinery.

### 2026-07-04 BlackThrush - read_parquet exact-width Arrow slice-copy NO-SHIP: 0.93x regression
Targeted the remaining `read_parquet` all-valid numeric residual (prior clean row: 62.1ms vs pandas/pyarrow 32ms =
0.52x) after `/alien-graveyard` pointed at vectorized execution with Arrow zero-copy interop. Hypothesis: exact-width
Arrow `Int64Array` and `Float64Array` arms still used `values().iter().map(|&v| v as T).collect()`, so replacing the
identity cast with `values().to_vec()` might let Rust lower the conversion to a tighter slice copy while leaving the
widening/narrowing arms untouched.

Measured on `/tmp/bench_num.parquet` with `crates/fp-io/examples/bench_parquet_num.rs` (1M x 6 numeric, best-of-6),
`AGENT_NAME=BlackThrush CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/data/projects/.rch-targets/frankenpandas-blackthrush-parquet-slicecopy`.
The literal requested `rch exec -- cargo bench --release -p fp-io` was run and Cargo rejected `--release` for `bench`;
the valid per-crate gate `rch exec -- cargo bench -p fp-io` completed green (fp-io has no measured bench fns, 605
ignored). `rch exec` for the fixture-bound example reached a worker but failed after compile because `/tmp/bench_num.parquet`
was not present there, so the timing A/B below is local fixture-bound execution in the same dedicated target setup.

| workload | current main | exact-width `to_vec()` candidate | ratio | pandas note |
| --- | ---: | ---: | ---: | --- |
| `read_parquet` 1M x 6 numeric | 72.49ms | 78.12ms | 0.93x REGRESSION | prior clean pandas/pyarrow 32ms makes this 0.44x -> 0.41x |

An earlier same-target single run looked like a tiny 72.65ms -> 69.52ms gain, but the clean rebuild A/B did not
reproduce it. Patch was reverted; no source change shipped. Conclusion: LLVM already handles the identity iterator
well enough, and the real residual remains the structural Arrow-buffer boundary: fp-columnar owns `Vec`/`Arc` typed
storage and does not depend on Arrow buffers. The next viable lever is an explicit foreign-buffer/Arrow-backed column
variant or ownership-transfer interface, with row-isomorphism tests and all typed accessors wired through it; that is a
multi-site storage design, not a safe single-loop perf commit.

### 2026-07-04 BlackThrush - read_parquet file-byte ownership NO-SHIP: ~1.02x FP-side, still 0.32x vs pandas
Targeted the same remaining `read_parquet` all-valid numeric residual after the exact-width Arrow slice-copy probe above.
Alien-graveyard mapping: vectorized execution / Arrow boundary minimization; artifact-coding proof obligation: preserve borrowed
`read_parquet_bytes(&[u8])` semantics while testing whether file-backed `read_parquet(path)` can avoid one redundant file-buffer
copy. Current code reads `Vec<u8>` from disk, then `read_parquet_bytes(&data)` clones it into `bytes::Bytes`; candidate factored a
private owned-bytes helper so `read_parquet(path)` moved the `Vec<u8>` into `Bytes` directly while leaving the public bytes API
unchanged. This only removes one 23 MB input copy for `/tmp/bench_num.parquet`; it does not change Arrow decode or Arrow-buffer ->
FrankenPandas owned-column conversion.

locally after RCH reported `no admissible workers: critical_pressure=1,insufficient_slots=10` (fp-io has 605 ignored tests and 0

fixture because remote workers do not have that `/tmp` file.

| workload | ORIG current main | owned-bytes candidate | pandas 2.2.3 | ratio |
| --- | ---: | ---: | ---: | --- |
| `read_parquet` 1M x 6 numeric | 73.22 ms / 75.83 ms | 73.84 ms / 71.86 ms | 22.75 ms | best candidate **1.02x FP-side** and **0.32x vs pandas** |

Verdict: NO-SHIP / reverted. The tiny best-case 73.22 -> 71.86 ms move is noise-scale and does not affect the dominant floor:
Parquet decode plus Arrow-buffer -> owned `Column` gathering. Avoiding the file-byte clone is too small for a real measured commit;
the viable radical lever remains an Arrow/foreign-buffer-backed column representation or a file/reader API that keeps decoded Arrow
buffers alive through typed consumers.

all-valid Float64 Arrow gathers into `Column::from_f64_values_owned`, but `Column::as_f64_slice_with_validity` did not accept the
resulting `LazyAllValidFloat64Vec` backing. Candidate added that witness arm so read-from-Parquet Float64 columns could enter

### 2026-07-04 Codex — RangeIndex bulk get_indexer precomputes bounds once (3.31x fp-side local fallback)
`RangeIndex::get_indexer` / `get_indexer_non_unique` / fallback `reindex` already reused `source_len`, but every target still recomputed the representable first/last bounds via widened arithmetic before doing the cheap step-membership test. FIX: precompute `(lower, upper)` once per bulk call and feed it to the same checked per-value arithmetic helper; if the endpoint arithmetic overflows, the helper keeps the original i128 fallback. Bit-identical: same bounds, same `checked_sub` / `checked_rem` / `checked_div`, same `-1` missing positions.

Measured against a clean `origin/main` worktree in `/data/projects/.scratch/frankenpandas-codex-range-bounds-baseline-20260704`, focused per-crate bench:
`CARGO_TARGET_DIR=.../.rch-targets/local-codex-fpindex-20260704 AGENT_NAME=Codex cargo bench -p fp-index --bench range_index_indexers current_get_indexer_miss_heavy -- --sample-size 10 --measurement-time 2`.
RCH required-remote runs were attempted first, but worker affinity was ignored and selected `hz2`, `vmi1149989`, `vmi1227854`, and a noisy `vmi1167313`, so the accepted proof is the same-machine local fallback. RCH sanity still showed the after path in the normal remote band (`vmi1149989`: 1M 2.5835ms; `vmi1227854`: 1M 2.6129ms).

| RangeIndex get_indexer miss-heavy | original clean main | after | ratio |
| --- | ---: | ---: | ---: |
| 100k targets | 1.5215ms | 0.30713ms | **4.95x fp-side WIN** |
| 1M targets | 12.653ms | 3.8230ms | **3.31x fp-side WIN** |

This is a production lever, not a bench-only artifact: the public bulk APIs now avoid O(n_targets) repeated endpoint derivation while preserving the wide-overflow path. Landing note: the source fast path is restored on `main` with the measured ratio above.

### 2026-07-04 BlackThrush — SeriesGroupBy by a Datetime64/Timedelta64 KEY: dense temporal arm — 6.7-7.6x fp-side + correctness fix (was Utf8("NaN") index)
SeriesGroupBy on a temporal KEY Series (`vals.groupby(&datetime_key).sum()`) fell to the generic per-row Scalar + SipHash build_groups path: `compute_dense_group_ids`/`dense_group_labels` handled Int64 (dense+open-addr) and Utf8 but had NO Datetime64/Timedelta64 arm (ns are i64 but `as_i64_slice` is Int64-gated). Two failures at 2M rows / 490k groups: (1) ~3.6s (0.0x); (2) the output index was mislabeled `Utf8("NaN")` instead of a DatetimeIndex. The DataFrameGroupBy sibling (groupby by a Datetime64 COLUMN) ALREADY had the correct temporal fast path (FxHashMap<i64,gid> → ScalarKey::Datetime64) — SeriesGroupBy was the uncovered sibling.
FIX: factored the two existing i64 arms into a shared `dense_gids_from_i64(&[i64])` helper (dense-histogram if bounded, else open-addr splitmix — the wide-i64 primitive that WINS vs khash) and added a temporal arm to `compute_dense_group_ids` (via `as_datetime64_slice()`/`as_timedelta64_slice()`, bail on any i64::MIN/NaT so dropna stays correct) + a matching `dense_group_labels` arm emitting `IndexLabel::Datetime64`/`Timedelta64`. ONE change flips every dense consumer (sum/mean/max/min/count/var/std/cum*/transform). Existing i64/Utf8 arms untouched.
| SeriesGroupBy Datetime64 key 2M/490k | BEFORE | AFTER | fp-side |
| .sum()   | 3610.8ms | 541.8ms | 6.7x |
| .mean()  | 3687.8ms | 522.3ms | 7.1x |
| .count() | 3619.0ms | 473.4ms | 7.6x |
Correctness: output index now `Datetime64(...)` (was `Utf8("NaN")`); group count 490872 MATCHES the proven DataFrameGroupBy temporal path exactly. fp-frame test suite GREEN (cargo test exit 0 — no test locked the old buggy Utf8 behavior, confirming it was untested). vs pandas: the open-addr wide-i64 machinery this reuses is measured 1.84x WIN on wide-i64 keys (seriesgroupby-wide-i64-key-khash-floor); Datetime64 ns are wide-i64, so this flips a ~0.05x loss to a likely ~1.8x WIN (absolute pandas ratio not re-measured — box was ~4x loaded, e.g. reindex read 1343ms warm vs the 288ms quiet baseline; the 6.7-7.6x fp-side ratio is load-robust). LESSON (again): a temporal KEY is just an i64 key with a different label variant — every i64 groupby/join/index fast path needs a Datetime64/Timedelta64 sibling; check the DataFrameGroupBy vs SeriesGroupBy pair for asymmetry.

### 2026-07-04 BlackThrush - Series.between nullable-f64 invalid-slot guard NO-SHIP: 0.89x FP-side, still 0.56x vs pandas
Targeted the residual nullable `Series::between` gap after the prior SIMD fast path made all-valid Float64 a win but left
nullable Float64 slower than pandas. Current code computes the raw f64 interval predicate in a vectorizable pass, then clears
invalid validity ranges to `false` so missing rows match pandas. Alien-graveyard mapping: vectorized execution plus sparse
validity sidecar; artifact-coding proof obligation: never treat an invalid slot's raw payload as present. Candidate first
scanned invalid ranges to prove every invalid raw payload already evaluated false; only if an invalid payload could match did it
run the existing clear pass. This is correct for arbitrary `LazyNullableFloat64` payloads, including arithmetic outputs where
invalid slots are not guaranteed to be the constructor's 0.0 sentinel.

Gates: required `AGENT_NAME=BlackThrush CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/data/projects/.rch-targets/pandas-cod rch exec -- cargo bench --release -p fp-frame`
was attempted and Cargo rejected `--release` for `bench`. Short per-crate timing used
`AGENT_NAME=BlackThrush CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/data/projects/.rch-targets/pandas-cod rch exec -- cargo run -q -p fp-frame --example bench_between_bt --release`.
The clean remote baseline on `vmi1149989` was `between_nullable 8.08ms`; post-patch rch had no admissible workers and fell open
locally, so the accepted keep/reject proof is the same local target warm A/B below. The candidate was reverted before commit.

| workload | ORIG current main | invalid-slot guard candidate | pandas 2.2.3 | ratio |
| --- | ---: | ---: | ---: | --- |
| `Series.between` nullable Float64, 2M rows / 20% NaN | 6.49 ms | 7.32 ms | 3.66 ms | **0.89x FP-side REGRESSION**, **0.56x vs pandas ORIG** |

Verdict: NO-SHIP / reverted. Reading invalid payloads and branching once per invalid run costs more than simply clearing the
random sparse invalid slots after the vectorized predicate pass. Do not retry "prove-invalids-already-false" for this workload;
the remaining gap is the boolean output/validity representation itself (pandas writes a compact native bool/NumPy mask, while
FrankenPandas emits `Vec<bool>`/typed Bool and still pays validity sidecar traversal).

### 2026-07-05 BlackThrush - Series.between nullable-f64 packed-validity fused predicate NO-SHIP: 1.00x FP-side, still 0.56x vs pandas
Targeted the same nullable `Series::between` residual after checking scratch/worktree wins. The remaining hot shape is not
another invalid-payload proof: it is the tradeoff between preserving the vectorized raw f64 predicate and applying the validity
sidecar. Alien-graveyard mapping: vectorized execution with a sparse validity sidecar; extreme-optimization proof obligation:
same `valid_bit && predicate(value)` result for every row, with missing rows forced to `false`.

Candidate fused the predicate with `ValidityMask::packed_words_for_scan()` so each output flag was produced as
`((word >> bit) & 1) == 1 && predicate(d[base + bit])`, skipping the separate `for_each_invalid_range` clear pass. This was
behavior-preserving, but it moved the validity branch back into the per-element loop and did not beat the current
vectorized-then-clear strategy on the short benchmark.

Short per-crate timing used the required target dir:
`AGENT_NAME=BlackThrush CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/data/projects/.rch-targets/pandas-cod rch exec -- cargo run -q -p fp-frame --example bench_between_bt --release`
on worker `hz2`. Candidate output was `between_nullable: 6.50ms` (`between_allvalid: 0.79ms`, `gt_allvalid: 0.61ms`,
`gt_nullable: 0.59ms`). ORIG/current-main comparator is the immediately preceding clean lane for the same workload,
`6.49ms`; pandas 2.2.3 comparator remains `3.66ms`.

| workload | ORIG current main | packed-validity fused candidate | pandas 2.2.3 | ratio |
| --- | ---: | ---: | ---: | --- |
| `Series.between` nullable Float64, 2M rows / 20% NaN | 6.49 ms | 6.50 ms | 3.66 ms | **0.998x FP-side**, **0.56x vs pandas** |

Verdict: NO-SHIP / reverted. Do not retry a fused validity-bit predicate for random sparse nullable Float64 `between`; the
branch inhibits the SIMD shape that the current implementation deliberately preserves. The viable frontier remains a different
Bool/validity representation or a policy-approved SIMD/target-feature path, not another rearrangement of the same scalar
validity bit inside the predicate loop.

### 2026-07-05 IronQuail — DataFrame axis=1 f64 reductions: tiled column-streaming kernel — 2.5x fp-side WIN (sum/mean/max/min/prod)

The row-axis Float64 reducers (`DataFrame::{sum,mean,max,min,prod}_axis1`, via `reduce_rows_f64` + `mean_axis1`'s
inline typed path) folded **row-at-a-time**: an outer `for row` loop with an inner `for col in &[&[f64]]` deref
(`acc = op(acc, col[row_idx])`). On a 1M x 10 Float64 frame that touches k=10 separate 8 MB column arrays per row
(column-strided — the reduction axis is orthogonal to the column-major storage), and the runtime-trip inner loop
over `&[&[f64]]` does NOT autovectorize, so it ran scalar at ~5 GB/s — latency-bound, far below DRAM bandwidth,
i.e. real headroom, not a bandwidth ceiling.

FIX (alien-graveyard: loop tiling / cache blocking + streaming AXPY): sweep a **row TILE of 4096** across all columns
so the per-tile accumulator stays L1-resident and each column's tile slice becomes a sequential, autovectorizable
`dst op= src` pass (addpd / maxpd / minpd / mulpd on baseline SSE2 — the release-perf profile has NO target-cpu
override). BIT-IDENTICAL: for a fixed row r the columns are folded left-to-right in the identical order with the same
`op` and `empty` seed — vectorization parallelizes across DISTINCT rows (lanes), never reassociating a single row's
fold. Standalone micro-bench (`-C target-cpu=x86-64`, matching production): mean 15044->5466us, max 15190->6879us,
**0 bit-diffs** vs the row loop.

Interleaved same-box A/B (ORIG clean-main binary vs candidate binary, best-of-6 p50, 1M x 10 f64):

| workload | ORIG main | tiled cand | fp-side | vs pandas 2.2.3 |
| --- | ---: | ---: | ---: | --- |
| `df_mean_axis1` | 16623.9us | 6581.0us | **2.53x** | ~9.9x (pandas 65342us) |
| `df_max_axis1`  | 16672.1us | 6707.4us | **2.49x** | ~9.4x (pandas 62820us) |
| `df_prod_axis1` | 16463.0us | 6479.6us | **2.54x** | ~19.9x (pandas 129119us) |
| `df_var_axis1` (control) | 19306.5us | 19251.6us | 1.00x | untouched (`reduce_rows_func_f64`) |
| `df_std_axis1` (control) | 20671.5us | 20730.8us | 1.00x | untouched |
| `df_median_axis1` (control) | 105740.7us | 106649.5us | 0.99x | untouched |

`sum_axis1`/`min_axis1` (not in fp-bench) get the same win through the shared `reduce_rows_f64`. GREEN: 79 axis1 lib
tests + full fp-frame suite pass; bit-identical. **LANDED on main.**

LESSON: a DataFrame reduction whose axis is orthogonal to storage (axis=1 over column-major f64) is BOTH cache-strided
AND blocks vectorization. Tile the rows + stream columns to convert it into sequential SIMD passes with an L1-resident
accumulator, without changing the per-row fold order (so it stays bit-identical). Open siblings on the same vein:
`reduce_rows_func_f64` (var/std/sem/skew axis=1 — needs a tiled two-pass moment kernel: tiled row-sum -> row-means,
then tiled `(col - mean)^2` accumulation) and `reduce_rows_int64`.

### 2026-07-05 BlackThrush - DataFrame var/std/sem(axis=1) tiled two-pass moment kernel NO-SHIP: 0.975x FP-side

Targeted the open sibling from the landed axis=1 f64 tiled fold: `reduce_rows_func_f64` for row sample moments. This is a
different primitive than the shipped simple fold kernel: a cache-blocked two-pass moment plan, first streaming each column
tile into per-row sums, then streaming columns again to accumulate each row's `m2 = sum((x - mean)^2)`.

Alien-graveyard mapping: cache-blocked/vectorized execution for an axis orthogonal to columnar storage. Alien-artifact
proof obligation: preserve pandas/FP sample-variance semantics by keeping each row's first-pass sum in column order and
each row's second-pass squared-deviation sum in the same column order; only lanes across different rows may vectorize.

Candidate added a guarded all-valid Float64 helper for `var_axis1`, `std_axis1`, and `sem_axis1`; nullable/mixed/generic
paths were untouched. It was fully reverted after measurement.

Short per-crate bench used the required target dir and same RCH worker:

`AGENT_NAME=BlackThrush CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/data/projects/.rch-targets/pandas-cod rch exec -- cargo run -q -p fp-frame --example bench_axis1 --release -- 1000000 10 var`

| workload | ORIG current main | tiled two-pass moment candidate | ratio |
| --- | ---: | ---: | ---: |
| `df.var(axis=1)`, 1M x 10 all-valid Float64 | 27.044924 ms | 27.741073 ms | **0.975x FP-side REGRESSION** |

Verdict: NO-SHIP / reverted. Do not retry the separate row-sums plus second-pass `m2` tile for all-valid f64 row variance:
the extra full output-sized sums buffer and second streaming pass erase the locality/vectorization benefit on this shape.
The viable frontier would need a different algebraic primitive with a stronger proof story, not this two-pass tiled helper.
### 2026-07-05 IronQuail — DataFrame.median(axis=1): branchless Batcher sorting-network per-row median — 2.3-3.6x fp-side WIN
